hypothesis:
## Novel Hypothesis

Adaptive hybrid batching that dynamically switches between static-64 and dynamic algorithms based on real-time monitoring of graph size variance within training epochs will reduce total training time by 15-30% compared to using either algorithm alone, particularly for datasets with non-stationary graph size distributions or curriculum learning schedules.

### Rationale

The paper demonstrates that algorithm performance depends on dataset characteristics, batch size, and number of training steps, with dynamic batching excelling early (fewer recompilations) and static-64 performing better later (lower per-step overhead). However, the paper only evaluates fixed algorithm choices throughout training. A hybrid approach that monitors the running variance of graph sizes in recent batches and switches algorithms when variance crosses empirically-determined thresholds could exploit the complementary strengths of both methods, especially for datasets where graph size distributions shift during training or when using data augmentation strategies that alter graph complexity over time.
